item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Both 135 and 2860 are fine cross-tests, it's clear why staff would try those experiments.
It's specifically the word "cognitohazardous" that doesn't work.
Looking at her tags, I just noticed that 135 isn't even a cognitohazard.

So my suggestion would be to remix the footnote to something more accurate.
"This includes the effects of phenomena normally dermatologically hazardous such as SCP-135 and SCP-2860."

That's a clunky phrasing, but it would be the smallest change possible that patches the problem.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Can you think of anything that might be a more suitable alternative?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm much too late to the party, but it makes no sense to test this with cognitohazards.

Even when it was a living person, the anomaly doesn't have a mental aspect. Test it with biohazards or any skips that cause injuries, the possibilities are wide open. But exposing it to crazy-making skips is like using heat-seeking missiles on an iceberg.

That incongruity was really jarring and had a serious negative impact on my enjoyment of the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how it shows the consequences of a popular superpower-type ability, +1.

item.title: Re: Testing
item.content:encoded: That makes it harder to contain so probably never.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

There are quite a few objects and links that can be added to this SCP article. To start, the mention of Site-77 can be made into a link to that Site's dossier. Additionally, at the line where they mention "Extensive testing has been conducted on SCP-1636's form, and it does not appear to be damageable by any means currently known to the Foundation" you could have a few examples of cognitohazard articles they have tested on 1636, such as SCP-135 or SCP-2860. Additionally, its not to far off base to compare 1636's invulnerability to that observed in SCP-2632, and propose similar origins of effect. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

02-07-1941 - SCP-1636 moved from Site-77 to Site-89 due to a containment breach in Site-77.

To this:

02-07-1941 - SCP-1636 moved from Site-77 to Site-89 due to a containment breach in Site-77.

Changing this:

Extensive testing has been conducted on SCP-1636's form, and it does not appear to be damageable by any means currently known to the Foundation.

To this:

Extensive testing has been conducted on SCP-1636's form, and it does not appear to be damageable by any means currently known to the Foundation1.

Changing this:

During initial containment, it was thought that the subject and SCP-1636 were a single entity.

To this:

During initial containment, it was thought that the subject and SCP-1636 were a single entity, with properties similar to those seen in SCP-2632.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. This includes the effects of normally cognitohazardous phenomena such as SCP-135, and SCP-2860.

item.title: Testing
item.content:encoded: When will the D-class testing start to see if the skin will latch to another subject?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Latency, the time from first exposure to manifestation of disease, is prolonged in the case of mesothelioma. It is virtually never less than fifteen years and peaks at 30–40 years. In a review of occupationally related mesothelioma cases, the median latency was 32 years.

(Wikipedia: Mesothelioma: Cause)

The subject was contained in 1934, and the handlers started showing mesothelioma in 1936.

I'm not sure if this is an intentional discrepancy, but it's definitely a horrifying one. It doesn't just give you cancer for completely sensible biological reasons- it does so at least seven times faster than anything we've found in nature. o.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus, worst life ever…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think this is the proper format for the content, as this reads more like a creepypasta, or a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is now part of my head-canon.

The "laws" of physics in the Foundation-verse are made to be broken; but above those are the inviolate Laws of Meta-Physics which even the most egregious anomalies obey. One of them is the Law of Conservation of Total Mass. Matter may not be created or destroyed without an equal and opposite destruction or creation of mass

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
or feed 'em to Walter. Or the Antishark. Or the Chocolate Fountain. If we had some way of making them not indestructible, we could even feed 'em to Josie.

I'm pretty sure that the sum of all the SCPs that create matter ex nihilo, plus the sum all the SCPs that make matter disappear, is zero.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Wait.

So because this thing exists.

The Foundationverse Earth is doomed to die in a lung-shredding sea of skin.

Unless we put the flakes somewhere else.

Like through the Glory Hole.

FFFFFFFUUUUUUUUUUUUU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a subtle but implacable XK scenario hidden in the details here.

Think of every eco horror story you've ever heard about styrofoam choking the oceans with non-biodegradable litter. Compared to these skin flakes, petroleum products are about as durable as mayfly farts.
4 kilograms per year, give or take, of utterly indestructible particulate contaminant, just accumulating and accumulating. Forever. Dunes and drifts and mounds and mountains of dandruff. The last generations of man will huddle on the highest peaks, the only land not swallowed by the endless grey sea of skin flakes, and they will regard the stories of a time when the world was not consumed by dust as the senile daydreams of foolish old men.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is like one of those twisted genie type things, with the unintended consequences of somehting normally considered desirable. A good subversion of the norm. I was worried for a minute when i read "indestructible," but it proved to be excellent.

I would love to know (actually, i would probably be horrified, but you get my point) how the insides were "ejected" without tearing the skin somewhere to fit it through. It would also be interestign to know what exactly killed him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was neutral, then upvoted with the acne. Well-explored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. People forget about the little details of so-called "superpowers".

"I want invincible skin!"
"And if you need surgery?"
"…well, shit…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Credit to my associate Roget who did the idea a hundred times better than I ever could and deserves a thousand dollars for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like that the internal body was ejected. I was kind of hoping for something like that.

I think the most interesting part of this is the creation of it, how it was a dude's skin and it's still going without him. Most of the rest didn't add much for me, so I'm going to have to think about this for a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, that's like the funniest thing ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said before, I don't really like the part about ejecting the internal body. But the rest is handled very well. Good article, Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: >.> I have a vast support network of cool kids with attitude who help me write things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, so many questions left unanswered. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ahem

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Voct helped too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wave wave

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that's kinda exactly what lured me in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i didn't write anything here and you cant prove i did

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is a mundane idea carried out _extremely_ well. The implications are well-thought-out and clever. This should be the standard for, "There are no bad ideas, only bad implementations."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Credit to my associate Gargus for giving me the inspiration for this guy. You should give him a hundred dollars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was sure you would have named this SCP "Achilles" but that's just me.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this, but I wish it was explored more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice follow-through on the implications of having an indestructible skin. I also like the mild creepiness of having a human skin lying around that still sheds skin flakes and is still at body temperature. Nice. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding fantem with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't say the actual object was boring. I feel it set the mood pretty well, because when I read 'indestructible epidermis' and not 'indestructible human', I was actually kinda interested as to why the hell we had just the guy's skin. Got my answer… Yeesh.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright. Just read it again, and I think I must have misinterpreted it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have indestructible skin flakes in the lung tissue. Your immune cells identify it as foreign biological matter and attack it. Since they cant break it down, the immune cells die and form scar tissue. As the scar tissue builds up, the lungs are damaged, leading to mesothelioma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the cells, the flakes of skin are basically doing the same thing that asbestos does. The skin retains the indestructibility when it flakes off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that, but that just isn't how skin cells, indestructible or not, would effect lung tissue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "item" itself is really boring at first glance, but the log is great. I think it deserves an upvote. I think this goes for a more emotional impact, I would hate to be that guy.

EDIT: Upon further reflection I think what did it for me is when it described the cysts underneath the skin, as someone who is still getting acne and pimples, this made me shudder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It gave them cancer because the skin flakes got in there lungs and caused severe scarring. This is the exact same thing asbestos does to cause mesothelioma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding Scantron's praise, but it's not enough to drive home an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could use a solid proofreading, but honestly? I like how you took the idea and explored it thoroughly over time. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
10-20-1946
Currently, SCP-1636's requests have been primarily for books and videos

I don't think that a child born sometime in the 1930s would ask for videos, especially considering that in the 1940s you didn't really have any easily mobile way to watch them. Bit of an anachronism there. Normally this wouldn't be a big deal, but I think it says something about the article in general- there seems to be a general lack of care and effort here. It looks like something cobbled together fast just for the sake of having another SCP, and because of this it fails to do anything worth while, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its another indestructible thing, except this time it gives you cancer, for some reason.
